evidence: Package layering is consistent: model and utils have no upward dependencies; config and query depend on model/utils; exploration depends on config and query; api depends on exploration, config, and query. No cycles visible in imports.
LongIntHashMap and LogDiceCalculator are correctly placed in utils/ — no domain dependencies, used across query and indexer subsystems.

RadialPlot (utils/RadialPlot.java) is an SVG rendering class that imports nothing from utils/ and is only consumed by api/VisualizationHandlers.java. Rendering logic is co-located with general utilities rather than with the api subsystem that owns it.

WordSketchApiServer (api/) directly constructs SemanticFieldExplorer (exploration/) inline in its constructor: `this.semanticFieldExplorer = new SemanticFieldExplorer(executor, grammarConfig)`. There is no injection seam; the api package owns the construction of an exploration-layer object.

RelationType enum in model/ serves as both a domain type and a parsing discriminator in config/GrammarConfigLoader (parsing 'SURFACE', 'DEP', 'ADJ_PREDICATE', etc.). The enum sits at the model layer but drives routing logic in the config layer above it.
RelationUtils was moved to config/ in this revision — that placement is correct. No residual boundary violations from the move.

No hub module: each package owns a bounded slice of the domain (corpus API, grammar config, semantic exploration, corpus index, query execution, shared model). The query/ package is the most cross-cut but doesn't import from api/ or exploration/.

Packages map clearly to domain capabilities: config (grammar rules), query (corpus search), exploration (semantic field analysis), model (domain DTOs), api (HTTP endpoints), indexer (corpus ingestion), utils (shared primitives). A new engineer can explain why each package exists.
model/package-info.java documents the naming conventions used by the package (records use Java record conventions, traditional classes use get-prefixes) — self-documenting structure.

GrammarConfig and GrammarConfigLoader are correctly separated: the value object (GrammarConfig) is independent of the I/O machinery (GrammarConfigLoader), letting callers depend only on the data they need.
ExploreResponseBuilder is package-private within api/ — its responsibility is translation from model to JSON maps; it does not escape the api boundary.

SemanticFieldExplorer now delegates entirely to MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator. Its own method body count is minimal: exploreMultiSeed calls multiSeedExplorer.explore(...), exploreByPattern calls a private helper chain, compareCollocateProfiles enforces a pre-condition then calls comparator. The facade is thin enough that a developer reading the exploration package must trace through two files to understand what 'exploration' does.

RelationType enum in model/ carries values like SURFACE, DEP, ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF. The first two are parsing/query-strategy markers, the last four are domain semantic relations. These two roles are merged into one enum, which means a change to the parsing strategy naming would touch a domain object.
CLAUDE.md documents the system architecture and all classes with their responsibilities — the documented structure matches the actual runtime structure.
QueryExecutor has 7 declared methods and exactly one production implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor). It is a single-implementation interface.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed(Set, RelationConfig, ExploreOptions) delegates in one line to multiSeedExplorer.explore(seeds, relationConfig, opts.minLogDice(), opts.topCollocates(), opts.minShared()). The non-deprecated overload adds only parameter unpacking.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern(String, RelationConfig, ExploreOptions) delegates to exploreByPattern(String, String, String, String, ExploreOptions) — but that 5-param overload is also public, leaking a lower-level API surface through the abstraction.

CollocateQueryHelper is a package-private helper that encapsulates complex ranking, hit-collection, and scoring logic shared by 4 methods in BlackLabQueryExecutor. It pays for itself: without it, BlackLabQueryExecutor would have ~400 extra lines.
ExploreOptions bundles 4 parameters (topCollocates, nounsPerSeed, minLogDice, minShared) into a record — justified given the parameter count and multiple callers.

ExploreResponseBuilder has real formatting, rounding, and edge-case logic (formatAdjectiveProfile with isSpecific/strongestNoun branching, buildEdges with type discrimination). It is not a pass-through.
GrammarConfigLoader is a pure loader (no state, no instance) returning immutable GrammarConfig values. The split between loader and value is a real abstraction boundary.
scan evidence: one_impl_interface_count=1 (QueryExecutor), facade_module_count=0, delegation_heavy_class_count=0. The mechanical signals confirm the observation.

The 5-parameter public overload of exploreByPattern (seed, relationName, bcqlPattern, simplePattern, opts) was originally meant as the low-level entry point but is now both public and accessible alongside the higher-level RelationConfig overload — callers must choose between two levels of abstraction on the same class.

pom.xml production runtime dependencies: blacklab-engine + blacklab-query-parser (same library family, distinct concerns: index/query engine vs. CQL/BCQL parsing), fastjson2 (single JSON library, no competing JSON dependency), slf4j-api + logback-classic (standard facade+implementation pair, no competing logging framework), jspecify (null-annotation library, no competing annotation dependency).

blacklab-engine is used for BlackLabIndex, HitGroups, Concordances and related BlackLab search types throughout the query subsystem. blacklab-query-parser provides CorpusQueryLanguageParser and ContextualQueryLanguageParser — two genuinely different parsers for two different query syntaxes. This is not duplication.
No multiple HTTP client libraries: the server uses com.sun.net.httpserver (JDK built-in) exclusively. No third-party HTTP server or client.
No duplicate date/time libraries, no competing serialization frameworks, no competing assertion libraries.
Test dependencies: junit-jupiter-api, junit-jupiter-engine, junit-platform-launcher — a single coherent JUnit 5 stack. No TestNG or Mockito competing alongside it.
logback 1.5.18 paired with slf4j 2.0.7 — compatible versions (SLF4J 2.x pairs with Logback 1.4+). No version conflict.
fastjson2 is used consistently across all JSON serialization call sites (HttpApiUtils, SketchHandlers, VisualizationHandlers, GrammarConfigLoader). No Jackson or Gson used anywhere.
holistic_context.dependencies shows no signals for dependency issues.
dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: Minor: jspecify (null annotations) is a compile-time annotation dependency with no runtime impact, but its presence alongside the JDK's @Nullable from other packages is worth watching as the annotation ecosystem evolves. No concrete defect found.

evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern is decomposed into 4 named phases (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterCandidates, identifyCoreCollocates) with clear single-responsibility. Each method is correctly scoped and named.
CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery uses a three-phase pipeline (collect → score → rank) with named intermediate records (HitRecord) and extracted private methods. The flow is linear and readable.

GrammarConfigLoader splits parsing into small private methods (parseAndValidateVersion, validateNoLegacyKeys, parseRelations, parseRelation, validatePositions) — the main parse() method reads as a sequence of named steps.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore: `ExplorationResult result;
result = semanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern(seed, resolvedConfig, opts);` — declaration and assignment are on separate lines with no intermediate state. The same split-declare pattern appears in handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti for ExplorationResult.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison: `ComparisonResult result;
result = semanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles(seeds, minLogDice, topCollocates);` — identical split-declare pattern for a third handler.

SketchHandlers.handleRelationQueryForPattern wraps executor.executeSurfacePattern in its own try-catch for IOException, sending a 500 response, then returns. This handler is itself called from wrapHandler (through handleSketchRequest), meaning IOException would be caught at the outer layer too. The inner catch adds specific error message text but introduces dual error-handling paths for the same failure mode.

BlackLabQueryExecutor.findCollocations collects frequencies via collectFrequenciesFromGroups with a lambda extractor. The approach is clean but the lambda `identity -> { ... return collocateLemma.isEmpty() ? null : collocateLemma; }` duplicates the null-vs-empty check pattern that appears in a slightly different form in collectDependencyFrequencies.
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens uses depth-counting with a quoted-string skip — correct and precise. The logic is clear and well-commented.

CollocateProfileComparator.buildAdjectiveProfileList manually computes variance, avg, max, min with raw array operations. This is intentional (no external stats library), but the four separate stream passes over the same `scores` array make it slightly harder to read than a single-pass accumulation.
ValidatedExploreRequest record bundles query parameters, resolved RelationConfig, and resolved ExploreParams after validation — a clean handoff object that eliminates re-validation in each handler.

ExploreResponseBuilder.populateExploreResponse(Map<String,Object>, ExplorationResult) takes a caller-owned mutable map and adds fields to it. The caller then adds more fields after the call (e.g., 'seed', 'seeds', 'seed_count'). This is a mutation-at-a-seam pattern: the caller must know which keys the method populates and which keys to add after.

buildBaseExploreResponse returns a mutable HashMap. handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti both add keys to the returned map after calling this method. The implicit contract is that callers must always mutate the returned map to complete it — there is no way to produce a complete response without mutation.
ExploreResponseBuilder.formatAdjectiveProfile returns a new Map<String,Object> rather than mutating an input — consistent with pure conversion, correct seam design.

handleSemanticFieldComparison does not go through validateExploreRequest (correct, since it has different semantics) but re-parses params and re-calls resolveExploreParams independently. The shared preamble pattern (resolveExploreParams, parseBaseExploreRequest) is duplicated across the handler and the shared validateExploreRequest path.

handleSemanticFieldExamples calls resolveRelationConfig but not resolveExploreParams or validateExploreRequest — it has its own param extraction for `top` (Integer.parseInt with manual 400 on NumberFormatException). The same parse-or-400 pattern is implemented a slightly different way than in resolveExploreParams.
Data translation from model to JSON is correctly isolated in ExploreResponseBuilder — no JSON map construction appears in SemanticFieldExplorer or the model layer.

Error propagation across the api→exploration seam: IAE from the exploration layer propagates up through the handler into wrapHandler which maps it to 400. This is a clean, documented contract (wrapHandler Javadoc specifies IAE→400).

SemanticFieldExplorer class-level Javadoc is substantive: explains exploration algorithm in a numbered algorithm outline with a concrete 'house' example, describes computational limits, and cross-references result DTOs. This is genuine documentation of a non-trivial algorithm.
LogDiceCalculator.compute Javadoc explains the UNCOMPUTABLE sentinel and its NaN semantics — not obvious from the signature alone. Adds value.

ExploreOptions field-level Javadoc: each field has a 1-line description. The comment_ratio is high (0.70) but the comments add non-trivial context (e.g., `nounsPerSeed` note about single-seed-only applicability).
RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES Javadoc lists all valid case-insensitive keys and states the pass-through behavior for unknown keys — concrete and useful. Comment_ratio 0.43 flagged by scanner.

GrammarConfigLoader private helpers: `missingConfigException` has `/** Returns a FileNotFoundException for a missing config file; always call with throw. */` — the 'always call with throw' note is genuine guidance for a non-obvious pattern.

ExplorationResult.empty(String) Javadoc: `@param seed the seed word; must not be null @return non-null empty result` — this accurately describes what requireNonNull enforces and what the return guarantees. The content is correct but the body (3 lines) is simple enough that the doc adds modest value.

Logger.debug calls in SemanticFieldExplorer are all guarded by `logger.isDebugEnabled()` where appropriate (CollocateProfileComparator.logTopProfiles has an explicit guard). No full-object dumps at INFO or WARN level.
No entry/exit trace logging on ordinary methods — logging is reserved for meaningful lifecycle events (exploration start/complete) and unexpected empty-results cases.

No defensive null-check on non-nullable typed values found: GrammarConfig.getRelations() returns an unmodifiable list, and callers trust it. No null checks on the return value of grammarConfig.getRelation(id).get() after isPresent() guard.

RelationEdgeType.toString() override is documented with a rationale: 'Overriding Object.toString() ensures that logging frameworks emit the API-safe label rather than the Java enum constant name'. This is a concrete 'why' explanation — not an echo comment.

Minor: `/** Build edges for graph visualization from a single-seed exploration result. */` on ExploreResponseBuilder.buildEdges(ExplorationResult) restates the method name with minimal additional context. Same for `/** Build edges for graph visualization from a comparison result. */` on the overload.
Minor: `private static String fmt(double value)` in RadialPlot has no Javadoc — consistent with its 'obvious from name' level. Appropriate absence of comment.
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: Two trivial restating Javadoc comments on buildEdges overloads in ExploreResponseBuilder and a few docstrings on very short factory methods where the code body is self-explanatory.

evidence: migration_signals reports 6 deprecated markers: 4 in SemanticFieldExplorer.java, 2 in CollocateProfileComparator.java.

SemanticFieldExplorer(QueryExecutor) is @Deprecated: 'Prefer SemanticFieldExplorer(QueryExecutor, GrammarConfig). Scheduled for removal in v2.0.' Active production code (WordSketchApiServer) uses the 2-arg constructor correctly. The deprecated constructor is not called by any production path.

CollocateProfileComparator(QueryExecutor) is @Deprecated: same pattern. The comparator is constructed with `new CollocateProfileComparator(executor, grammarConfig)` in SemanticFieldExplorer — the new path is used.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed(Set, RelationConfig, double, int, int) is @Deprecated: 'Prefer exploreMultiSeed(Set, RelationConfig, ExploreOptions). Scheduled for removal in v2.0.' ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti calls `semanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed(seeds, resolvedConfig, opts)` — the ExploreOptions overload — correctly.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern(String, String, String, String, ExploreOptions) is public but not @Deprecated, even though it is the lower-level internal overload that callers should bypass in favor of exploreByPattern(String, RelationConfig, ExploreOptions).

The deprecated 1-arg constructors delegate to their respective 2-arg constructors with null for GrammarConfig, activating FALLBACK_NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT and FALLBACK_ADJECTIVE_PATTERN respectively. These fallback constants are live code that diverges from the config-driven path.
No stale TODO/FIXME markers referencing 'migrate', 'legacy', 'old api', or 'remove after' found in the scanned files.

No mixed old/new API coexistence patterns beyond the documented @Deprecated symbols. RelationConfig.buildFullPattern(String headword, String collocateLemma) and buildFullPattern(String headword) are both present and active — neither is deprecated, and both serve distinct call sites.

ExploreOptions.nounsPerSeed is passed as 0 when constructing options for multi-seed exploration: `new ExploreOptions(topCollocates, 0, minLogDice, minShared)`. The Javadoc says nounsPerSeed 'is not used in multi-seed exploration'. This is a param survivor from the transition to ExploreOptions.

structure.directory_profiles lists 7 production packages: api/ (6 files, 1307 LOC), config/ (4 files, 591 LOC), exploration/ (4 files, 726 LOC), indexer/blacklab/ (2 files, 231 LOC), model/ (11 files including package-info), query/ (4 files), utils/ (4 files).
No package has more than 11 files. No flat-directory overload. Each package has a coherent surface explanation.
indexer/blacklab/ is correctly isolated as a 2-file subsystem (BlackLabConllUIndexer + ConlluConverter). Low file count, focused responsibility.
exploration/ (4 files) groups SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator, ExploreOptions cleanly by exploration algorithm. Navigation is predictable.

utils/ contains 4 files: CqlUtils (CQL parsing primitives), LogDiceCalculator (statistical scoring), LongIntHashMap (custom collection), RadialPlot (SVG renderer). CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, and LongIntHashMap are genuine utilities with no domain dependencies. RadialPlot is an SVG rendering engine for word-sketch visualization — it is only imported by api/VisualizationHandlers.java, not by any other utils/ consumer.

model/ contains 11 files including package-info. QueryResults defines SnippetResult and CollocateResult — two result types whose primary consumers are in query/ (BlackLabQueryExecutor) and api/ (SketchHandlers). These result types are in model/ because they are shared across the query and api layers, which is correct placement.

query/ contains BlackLabQueryExecutor, BlackLabSnippetParser, CollocateQueryHelper (package-private), QueryExecutor. The three non-public files correctly stay in the package; only QueryExecutor is public. This scoping discipline is correct.

api/ (6 files, 1307 LOC) is the heaviest package by LOC but the file count is manageable. All 6 files have clear roles: server wiring (WordSketchApiServer), HTTP utilities (HttpApiUtils), 3 handler groups (Sketch, Exploration, Visualization), and a response builder (ExploreResponseBuilder).

Main.java is at the root (word_sketch/) — it is the application entry point, fan_in=0 from other source files, and is correctly placed as a root-level file with no imports from utils/ and no utility role.

RelationConfig.buildFullPattern(String) is annotated @Nullable. Its Javadoc says it 'returns null when the pattern field is null'. GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelation validates that pattern is non-null and non-blank, throwing IOException if absent. So in practice the @Nullable return is theoretically unreachable in a correctly loaded config — but callers cannot know this without tracing through the loader.

SketchHandlers.handleRelationQueryForPattern calls `rel.buildFullPattern(lemma)` and passes the result directly to `executor.executeSurfacePattern(lemma, fullPattern, 0.0, 50)` without a null check. If buildFullPattern returned null (as its annotation permits), this would cause a NullPointerException in executeSurfacePattern rather than a clean 400 error.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore calls `resolvedConfig.relationType().orElseThrow()` after the validateExploreRequest path has already guarded against empty relationType (resolveRelationConfig returns null and sends 400 if relType.isEmpty()). The orElseThrow is unreachable but declares a partial-state possibility that doesn't exist at that call site. A reader must trace through two methods to confirm the Optional is always present.

QueryExecutor.findCollocations Javadoc: 'cqlPattern must start with ['. BlackLabQueryExecutor.findCollocations calls buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution which throws IllegalArgumentException for patterns not starting with [. The exception is documented at the interface level ('throws IllegalArgumentException if cqlPattern is not in a recognized format') — the contract is honored.

LogDiceCalculator.compute documents UNCOMPUTABLE = Double.NaN as the return for invalid inputs. Callers in CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates check Double.isNaN(logDice) before inclusion. The contract is consistently honored.
ExplorationResult.empty(String) enforces requireNonNull on seed and documents '@param seed the seed word; must not be null'. The contract is enforced at runtime. Consistent.
ComparisonResult.empty() returns an empty result — its Javadoc notes 'use getNouns().isEmpty() to detect an empty result'. No confusion between null return and empty result. Consistent.

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish wraps IOException in IllegalStateException. The method's Javadoc says 'Wraps any IOException in an IllegalStateException with the config path included'. Contract is documented and honored.

SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries catches both IOException and RuntimeException in the per-relation loop, accumulating errors in relationErrors. The Javadoc on the method says 'errors are collected in relationErrors rather than propagated'. The contract is documented and honored.

RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias is declared to return @Nullable and returns null when its input is null. Callers (ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig) pass the result to grammarConfig.getRelation() which accepts @Nullable implicitly through Map.get(). The null flows correctly but only because Map.get() tolerates null keys; this is consistent but depends on understanding the HashMap null-key contract.
Guard clauses are applied consistently and early: findCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern all return Collections.emptyList() when lemma is null/empty, avoiding deeper NPEs.

handleRelationQueryForPattern (SketchHandlers) contains an internal try/catch for IOException from executeSurfacePattern, yet the method is declared throws IOException and is invoked from a handler already wrapped by wrapHandler. The method signature advertises that it propagates IOException but swallows the one IOException path that actually fires in production.

ExploreResponseBuilder.formatAdjectiveProfile checks 'if (adj.nounScores() != null)' before iterating, but AdjectiveProfile.nounScores() is a record component constructed from a freshly-allocated LinkedHashMap that is never null — the check is always true and misleads readers about the type contract.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore uses a split declaration: 'ExplorationResult result; result = semanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern(...)' — no code separates the two statements, so the blank declaration communicates nothing except uncertainty.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern returns ExplorationResult.empty(seed != null ? seed : "") when seed is null or empty. The null check and empty check are fused into a single condition, then the ternary silently coerces null to "". A reader cannot tell at a glance which branch returns null vs. empty string.

BlackLabQueryExecutor.buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution: the if/else has the throw in the else branch, which is correct, but the Javadoc says 'must start with [' — the contract is clear and logic is sound here.

CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits: 'int limit = (int) Math.min((long) maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR, (long) records.size())' — wide cast avoids overflow before min(); clean logic with an in-code comment explaining safety.

The codebase uses org.jspecify.annotations.@Nullable on return types and parameters: HttpApiUtils.requireParam, RelationConfig.buildFullPattern, BlackLabSnippetParser methods, RelationConfig.getDeprel. This is a systematic, consistent adoption of nullable annotations on public APIs.

CollocateQueryHelper constructor declares 'CollocateQueryHelper(@Nullable BlackLabIndex index)' but no method in the class null-guards 'index' before use. getTotalFrequency calls 'index.mainAnnotatedField()' — a NPE if null is passed. The @Nullable annotation overstates the valid input domain.

QueryResults.CollocateResult record's 'collocateLemma' field is documented as '/* nullable */' in a comment in CollocateQueryHelper.HitRecord but the field in the CollocateResult record itself carries no @Nullable annotation. Callers (buildBcqlResponse in SketchHandlers) defensively check 'r.collocateLemma() != null' — indicating the nullable contract is understood but not declared.

Optional<RelationType> in RelationConfig is used consistently to represent absent relation_type, with callers always using .orElse() or .map(). This is a strong type-level representation of optionality.
GrammarConfig.getConfigPath() is @Nullable Path — the configPath can be null when loaded from a Reader (no file path). The annotation correctly warns callers.
BlackLabSnippetParser.extractHeadword returns @Nullable String — correct, the BCQL pattern may not contain a lemma attribute.
ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig and resolveExploreParams return @Nullable, and every caller checks immediately. The pattern is consistent.
No raw unchecked casts found in production code. Fastjson2 access uses typed getters (getString, getIntValue) rather than casting Object.
RelationType enum is used consistently; no raw string comparisons against enum names in production paths except in GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType which uses valueOf with a try/catch — safe.

Method names in QueryExecutor are precise and differentiated: findCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeBcqlQuery, executeCqlQuery, executeDependencyPattern — each name encodes both the operation and the expected input format.

ExploreOptions.nounsPerSeed misleads: the value is passed as 'nounsPerPredicate' in SemanticFieldExplorer.buildCollocateToNounsMap — the semantic is 'nouns to expand per collocate (adjective/predicate)', not 'nouns per seed'. The parameter name in the record and the local variable name in the algorithm disagree.

Abbreviation inconsistency between files: SemanticFieldExplorer uses 'seedCollocScores' (colloc = truncated), while MultiSeedExplorer uses 'seedCollocateMap' (collocate = full). Both appear in the same exploration subsystem and cross-reference each other.

BlackLabSnippetParser.extractCollocateLemma Javadoc claims it extracts the 'labeled 2:' lemma, but the implementation simply returns the last lemma attribute in the XML via extractLastLemma — no label awareness. The name implies position-sensitivity that does not exist.

HttpApiUtils has a stale Javadoc reference: 'setCorsHeader calls {link #CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN}' but that constant was replaced by getCorsAllowOrigin() (a method, not a field). The cross-reference points to a non-existent symbol.

CollocateProfileComparator.deriveAdjectivePattern vs SemanticFieldExplorer.deriveNounCqlConstraint: parallel methods named inconsistently ('pattern' vs 'constraint', 'adjective' vs 'noun' — fine) but also 'derive' vs 'derive' — this part is consistent.

In ExploreResponseBuilder, loop variable 'c' for CoreCollocate in formatCoreCollocates is too abbreviated for a non-trivial loop body: 'cm.put("word", c.collocate())' — 'cc' or 'coreColloc' would be clearer.

parseBaseExploreRequest in ExplorationHandlers: the 'Base' qualifier adds no semantic distinction — all four handlers parse the same exchange.getRequestURI().getQuery(). The name implies a hierarchy of parsing stages that doesn't exist.

ExploreOptions: the Javadoc note acknowledges 'Explore' vs 'Exploration' asymmetry intentionally. This transparency prevents the naming asymmetry from being a defect, but it remains a discoverability friction for new contributors.

wrapWithErrorHandling in HttpApiUtils is the canonical error boundary: IAE→400, IOException→500, Exception→500. All handler registrations in WordSketchApiServer.registerRoutes use wrapHandler, which delegates to wrapWithErrorHandling. This is a consistent, testable pattern.

SketchHandlers.handleRelationQueryForPattern contains an internal try/catch for IOException from executeSurfacePattern that bypasses wrapHandler: catches, logs with logger.error, then sends 'Query failed: <msg>'. The wrapHandler description-prefixed message ('Sketch request failed: <msg>') is never reached for this path. Two different 500-message formats exist for the same operation.

SketchHandlers.parseBcqlRequest uses three distinct error-signaling strategies within one method: throws IAE for bad JSON (propagates to wrapHandler→400), calls sendError+return null for missing/too-long/too-complex query (direct 400), returns null for body-too-large (direct 413). The inconsistency is contained but notable.

GrammarConfigLoader: IOException is propagated from load(Path); createDefaultEnglish wraps it in IllegalStateException with the config path in the message — context preserved, appropriate layer transition.

BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor: broad 'catch (Exception e)' wraps in IOException. The cause is passed, so context is preserved. The broad catch is justified (BlackLab throws heterogeneous runtime exceptions on index failure).
CollocateQueryHelper.getTotalFrequency: catches RuntimeException, wraps in IOException with lemma context. Cause preserved.

executeRelationQueries in SketchHandlers: catches 'IOException | RuntimeException' per relation and appends to 'relationErrors'. Errors are included in the response JSON rather than silently swallowed. Intentional and appropriate for a partial-results scenario.

ExplorationHandlers.resolveExploreParams: catches NumberFormatException and calls sendError(400, ...). No error context beyond 'expected an integer or decimal value' — the bad parameter name is not included in the message, losing diagnostic context.

SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles throws IAE for fewer than 2 seeds. The calling layer (ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti) validates this case before calling, so the IAE is a safety net rather than the primary error path — but the two layers have overlapping checks.

WordSketchApiServer constructor explicitly defers port binding to start(): 'NOTE: Port binding is deferred to start() so that construction and network binding are separated.' This is the correct pattern for testability and configuration-before-binding.

Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand registers the shutdown hook BEFORE constructing the server: 'AtomicReference<WordSketchApiServer> serverHolder = new AtomicReference<>()' is set up before the server is created, ensuring executor.close() is always called even if server construction throws. Careful initialization ordering.

HttpApiUtils.getCorsAllowOrigin() reads System.getProperty at call time, not at class-load time. The Javadoc explicitly calls this out: 'Read at use time via getCorsAllowOrigin() so that the system property is evaluated when the response is sent, not at class-load time.' This prevents the common fragility of reading system properties before they are set.
RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES is a static final Map initialized from Map.of() with string literals — no I/O, no ordering dependency, immutable at class load. Safe.
BlackLabSnippetParser static Pattern fields are compiled regex from string literals — deterministic, no external dependency at class load. Safe.

SemanticFieldExplorer(QueryExecutor, GrammarConfig) constructor: delegates immediately to CollocateProfileComparator(executor, grammarConfig) and MultiSeedExplorer(executor). All purely in-memory. No I/O at construction time.
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() reads System.getProperty at method call time, not class-load time, so the system property can be set after JVM start.

No circular initialization dependencies found: the dependency graph is a clean DAG (Main → WordSketchApiServer → {SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers} → {QueryExecutor, SemanticFieldExplorer} → {CollocateProfileComparator, MultiSeedExplorer, QueryExecutor}).
CollocateQueryHelper is constructed inside BlackLabQueryExecutor's constructor and receives the already-open BlackLabIndex — no lazy initialization, no global singleton, no ordering risk.
initialization_coupling.issues_preventing_higher_score: The only material concern is that CollocateQueryHelper accepts @Nullable BlackLabIndex in its constructor (discussed in type_safety batch) — if null were passed, every method would throw NPE. This is a latent coupling to construction order rather than a strict initialization_coupling defect, but it represents untested null-tolerance that could surface if the constructor is called from a new context.

evidence: Package structure is consistent: api/, config/, exploration/, indexer/blacklab/, model/, query/, utils/. Each package has a focused domain. No directories follow different organizational patterns.
Java naming conventions are uniformly applied: camelCase methods, PascalCase types, SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE constants throughout all files.

Access modifiers in the api package follow a consistent pattern: WordSketchApiServer is public, all handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, VisualizationHandlers, ExploreResponseBuilder, HttpApiUtils) are package-private. This correctly encapsulates the handler layer.

MultiSeedExplorer is package-private (no modifier) while CollocateProfileComparator is public class, both in the exploration/ package. Both are implementation classes for the SemanticFieldExplorer facade. There is no documented reason why one is public and the other is not.

Model class design strategy is intentionally mixed: ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult are traditional classes with get-prefixed accessors; AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExplorationResult are records with component accessors. The package-info.java explicitly documents this: 'traditional classes use get-prefixed accessors; records omit the prefix per Java record conventions.' The rationale is stated and the split is intentional.

Deprecated constructors/overloads follow a consistent pattern across both SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator: @Deprecated annotation, same-class delegation, Javadoc @deprecated note with removal timeline and preferred alternative. This is systematic.
Abbreviation 'colloc' (truncated) vs 'collocate' (full) in related exploration files — covered under naming_quality but also a convention outlier across sibling modules.

handleSemanticFieldComparison in ExplorationHandlers builds its response inline (direct HashMap puts) while handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti delegate to ExploreResponseBuilder.populateExploreResponse. Sibling handlers in the same class follow different response-assembly conventions.
BCQL endpoint uses 'limit' parameter name in the request body; GET sketch endpoints use 'top'. Both control max-results but use different names — a cross-endpoint naming drift.
Core logic is well-tested: CqlUtilsTest, LogDiceCalculatorTest, LongIntHashMapTest, BlackLabSnippetParserTest cover pure computational units with no external dependencies.
New round-16 additions are covered: WrapHandlerTest (4 tests), ExploreResponseBuilderTest (8 tests), MultiSeedExplorerTest (6 tests) — the highest-risk new code has unit tests.
SemanticFieldExplorerTest, CollocateProfileComparatorTest exist. RelationsValidationTest validates the grammar config JSON.
GrammarConfigLoaderTest and RelationUtilsTest cover the config layer.

BlackLabQueryExecutorTest, HybridAlgorithmTest, DependencySketchTest are integration tests requiring a real BlackLab index. These likely have @Disabled or @Tag annotations and run against a local corpus — not runnable in CI without an index fixture.

handleRelationQueryForPattern's internal IOException catch (identified in other batches) has no dedicated test. There is no test verifying that the '500: Query failed:' message format fires correctly for that specific path.
handleSemanticFieldComparison has no dedicated unit test. Its response shape (fully_shared_count, partially_shared_count, edges structure) is not verified outside an integration context.

SketchHandlers.handleFullSketch, handleDepSketchForType, handleFullSketchForType, executeRelationQueries have no unit tests — these are tested only indirectly through HandlersTest or integration tests.

No test for Main.java CLI argument parsing: --port, --index, --input/--output, error cases (missing --index, invalid --port). The CLI parsing in handleBlackLabServerCommand, handleBlackLabIndexCommand, handleBlackLabQueryCommand is entirely untested.
Test isolation is good where tests exist: MultiSeedExplorerTest uses a stub QueryExecutor with canned data, not a real BlackLab index.
No snapshot tests — all assertions are behavioral. This is a strength.
WrapHandlerTest depends on HandlersTest.MockExchange — test-class coupling that is acceptable but could be replaced with a standalone mock.

QueryExecutor interface parameter ordering is consistent: (lemma, pattern/deprel, minLogDice, maxResults) across findCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern, executeDependencyPatternWithPos. The headPosConstraint comes last in the 5-param variant — appropriate.

SemanticFieldExplorer public API has a clean shape after the round-16 work: exploreByPattern(seed, RelationConfig, ExploreOptions) and exploreMultiSeed(seeds, RelationConfig, ExploreOptions) are structurally parallel. The deprecated 5-param exploreMultiSeed still exists but is clearly marked.

HTTP GET endpoints consistently use 'top' for max-results: ?top=15. The POST /api/bcql endpoint uses 'limit' in the JSON body. Both control max-results but have different names — a client building a generic parameter layer would need to know which spelling to use for which endpoint.

HttpApiUtils static methods consistently put HttpExchange first, followed by data: sendJsonResponse(exchange, data), sendError(exchange, code, message), sendBinaryResponse(exchange, contentType, bytes). This is a clean convention applied uniformly.

POST /api/bcql response uses 'results' as the array key; GET /api/sketch/{lemma} uses 'collocations'; GET /api/semantic-field/explore uses 'seed_collocates'. Each endpoint's primary result array has a different key name. While semantically distinct, a caller building a generic results-renderer cannot reuse the same key path.

ExplorationHandlers has a 4-param public overload (seed, relationName, bcqlPattern, simplePattern, opts) on exploreByPattern in SemanticFieldExplorer — this is a lower-level overload that exposes pattern strings directly. The 3-param overload (seed, RelationConfig, opts) is the preferred API. The 4-param form is public and accessible, creating an overloaded surface where one variant should clearly be internal.
WordSketchApiServer endpoint listing in class Javadoc matches the registerRoutes method — documentation and implementation are in sync. Good.
ExploreResponseBuilder is package-private (api-internal only) — not part of the public API surface. Clean encapsulation.
validateExploreRequest private method in ExplorationHandlers elegantly consolidates shared preamble for three handlers into a single call, reducing the effective public surface complexity.

The server binds exclusively to loopback address: 'new InetSocketAddress(InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress(), port)' — the service is structurally inaccessible from the network, providing a strong architectural security boundary.
All endpoints are equally unauthenticated. There is no route that has auth middleware and a sibling that does not — the auth policy (none, loopback-only) is applied uniformly.

Input validation is consistently applied: POST endpoints have MAX_REQUEST_BODY_BYTES (65536) size limits in both SketchHandlers and VisualizationHandlers. GET endpoints validate required parameters via requireParam and numeric parameters with explicit NumberFormatException handling.

BCQL endpoint has three additional guards beyond body size: MAX_BCQL_PATTERN_LENGTH (1024 chars), MAX_BCQL_BRACKET_DEPTH (20 tokens), and JSON structure validation. These DoS-mitigation controls are applied consistently within the endpoint.

CORS is configured via a system property (cors.allow.origin, default localhost:3000). The setCorsHeader method is called by every response path (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendBinaryResponse, sendOptionsResponse) — no response can escape without CORS headers. Consistent.
No read/write separation: all endpoints are read-only corpus queries. There are no mutation endpoints, no admin routes, and no privilege levels to differentiate.
The /health endpoint does not require any validation beyond the GET method check — appropriate for a health check that returns only 'status: ok'.
No magic permission strings or role checks of any kind — not applicable for this tool's security model.
No audit logging — not applicable for a local research tool without user identity.
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: The CORS default (localhost:3000 only) is correct for local use but is not documented as an explicit security decision. A developer running this on a shared machine or behind a proxy might change cors.allow.origin to '*' without understanding the exposure. One minor gap: the loopback binding is not documented in the API Javadoc or README beyond a comment in the code.

evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern is a 4-phase algorithm with each phase delegated to a private named method (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterCandidates, identifyCoreCollocates). Each helper has a single responsibility. This is exemplary decomposition.

MultiSeedExplorer.explore is a ~90-line method handling the full multi-seed algorithm in one body: fetching collocates per seed, computing intersection, aggregating scores, building discovered-noun list, building core-collocate list. No phase helpers are extracted. The method does 5 distinct operations without decomposition.

SketchHandlers mixes three unrelated concerns: sketch queries (the primary domain), concordance examples (a supporting query feature), and arbitrary BCQL queries (a power-user escape hatch). These have different error contracts, different parameter shapes, and different response shapes. The class name 'SketchHandlers' does not predict the presence of BCQL or concordance functionality.

handleSemanticFieldComparison in ExplorationHandlers builds its response inline while the three sibling handlers delegate to ExploreResponseBuilder. The response for comparison is assembled in the handler (a presentation concern), violating the separation that ExploreResponseBuilder was created to enforce.

executeRelationQueries in SketchHandlers uses a BiFunction<RelationConfig, ExecutedSketch, Map<String, Object>> callback to share the relation-iteration loop between two dispatch paths. This is clever but creates an unusual indirection for Java — a developer following the two call sites must mentally evaluate an anonymous function to understand the output shape.

GrammarConfigLoader is well-decomposed: parse, parseAndValidateVersion, validateNoLegacyKeys, parseRelations, parseRelation, validatePositions — each does one thing. The public surface (load, fromReader, createDefaultEnglish) is minimal.

CollocateQueryHelper phases are cleanly named (executeCollocateSearch, collectHits, scoreHits, buildAndRankCollocates) and each is a focused private/package-private method. No single method exceeds its stated scope.

BlackLabQueryExecutor.collectFrequenciesFromGroups is a shared, well-parameterized helper used by four different query methods with a Function<String, String> lemmaExtractor. The design lets each query type supply its own collocate-extraction strategy without duplicating the iteration.

WordSketchApiServer.registerRoutes contains 10 route registrations that all follow the exact same structural pattern: registerXxxHandler(server, path, wrapHandler(...)). This is correctly data-driven in shape but could be further reduced to a table-driven approach. Not a blocker.

SketchHandlers.handleRelationQueryForPattern has 7 parameter-dependent dispatch branches via if/else chains on path splitting — the routing logic that WordSketchApiServer could handle is being done inside the handler method, mixing routing concern with handler concern.

strengths: Dependency direction flows consistently toward model/utils at the base; no downward imports (model never imports api or exploration).
RelationUtils correctly placed in config/ after recent move — no residual boundary drift.
indexer/blacklab/ is correctly isolated: it imports from model and utils but not from api or exploration, keeping indexing concerns out of the query-serving subsystem.
No circular dependencies detected across any of the 7 packages.
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: Issues are localized to two specific placement decisions (RadialPlot in utils/ and inline construction of SemanticFieldExplorer in api/) rather than systemic boundary violations or cycles.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The architecture is fundamentally sound with a clear layering story and no cycles. Two issues prevent a higher score: RadialPlot's placement in utils/ conflates rendering with general utilities (a boundary blurring that will confuse contributors looking for visualization code), and the api layer's direct construction of SemanticFieldExplorer introduces a tight coupling that eliminates the injection seam used elsewhere for testability. These are localized, fixable problems rather than structural failures, which places the score in the mid-70s: above 'significant drag' but short of 'solid with minor unevenness'.

strengths: Package-to-domain-capability mapping is clear and consistent — api, config, exploration, query, model, indexer, utils all have distinct, explainable roles.
GrammarConfig/GrammarConfigLoader separation cleanly decouples data from I/O — a textbook value-object + loader pattern.
ExploreResponseBuilder is correctly scoped as package-private inside api/, preventing presentation concerns from leaking into the model layer.
model/package-info.java documents accessor naming conventions explicitly, preventing inconsistency as the package grows.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are localized to a single abstraction (SemanticFieldExplorer) being thinner than the facade pattern justifies, and a dual-purpose enum (RelationType) blurring domain vs. structural concerns — neither is systemic but both add navigation friction.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase shows strong top-level decomposition with well-named packages, clear ownership, and a documented architecture that matches the code. What pulls the score into the upper-70s rather than 85+ is that SemanticFieldExplorer's value as a facade is now marginal (it adds one pre-condition check and delegates everything else), and RelationType conflates parsing-strategy markers with semantic relation types in the model layer. These are real clarity costs but not structural failures — the project remains navigable and professionally organized.

strengths: CollocateQueryHelper is a well-justified extraction: it owns hit-collection, collocate ranking, and BCQL two-phase pipeline, reducing BlackLabQueryExecutor from a god class to a coordinator.
ExploreResponseBuilder has genuine formatting logic (rounding, category labelling, optional specific-noun extraction) — it earns its existence.
ExploreOptions cleanly groups the 4-parameter tuple that previously spread across every exploration call site.
GrammarConfig (value) / GrammarConfigLoader (I/O) split is a textbook clean abstraction.
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: Issues are concentrated in the exploration facade layer: one single-impl interface and one class that exposes both high-level and low-level overloads of the same operation, giving callers more surface area than necessary.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The abstraction layer is mostly healthy: helpers are justified, options objects are appropriate, and the loader/value split is exemplary. The score falls into the upper-70s rather than 85+ because QueryExecutor is a wide (7 methods) single-implementation interface that imposes design overhead without current payback, and SemanticFieldExplorer leaks its internal 5-param overload as a public API alongside the higher-level RelationConfig overload — callers face an unnecessary choice. These are contained issues, not systemic over-abstraction.

strengths: Single JSON library (fastjson2) used consistently at all call sites — no competing serialization framework.
HTTP server uses the JDK built-in com.sun.net.httpserver — no third-party HTTP dependency required.
BlackLab engine and parser are distinct modules for distinct purposes (search execution vs. query parsing), not the same function duplicated.
Logging uses the standard SLF4J + Logback pair at compatible versions (slf4j 2.0.7 + logback 1.5.18).
JUnit 5 stack is self-consistent (api + engine + platform-launcher at matching versions).
dependency_health.issue_character: No dependency health defects identified; the dependency graph is minimal, purposeful, and free of duplication or version conflicts.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The dependency set is small and well-chosen. Every production dependency earns its weight: BlackLab is the core corpus search engine, fastjson2 handles JSON, SLF4J+Logback provides logging, jspecify adds null-annotation support. There are no duplicate libraries for the same purpose, no heavy deps pulled in for trivial use, and no version conflicts visible in the pom. The score is 90 rather than 100 because fastjson2's 'fast' serialization includes non-standard extension APIs (JSONObject, JSONArray mutation) that create a mild library lock-in — this is a design-time trade-off rather than a defect, but it is worth noting.

strengths: Phase-structured exploration methods (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterCandidates, identifyCoreCollocates) are each single-responsibility with clear names.
GrammarConfigLoader's private decomposition into parseAndValidateVersion, validateNoLegacyKeys, parseRelations, parseRelation — the main entry point reads as a clean recipe.
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens uses depth-counting with proper handling of nested brackets and quoted strings — precise edge-case handling without defensive sprawl.
LongIntHashMap is a clean, self-contained data structure with correct rehash logic and sentinel documentation.
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are patterns rather than one-off mistakes: split-declare appears in three different handler methods, and dual error-handling appears in one query method — suggesting a code-style drift during implementation rather than a single architectural choice.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase shows strong decomposition discipline and clear phase-based structuring. What pulls the score down into the mid-70s is the recurrence of the split-declare-then-assign pattern across multiple handlers (a minor but visible style inconsistency) and one case of redundant IOException handling inside a method that is already wrapped. These are refinement-level issues, not structural failures, so the score sits above 'significant drag' but below 'solid but uneven'.

strengths: ValidatedExploreRequest record cleanly encapsulates the post-validation state, eliminating repetitive re-validation across handlers.
Model-to-JSON translation is correctly isolated in ExploreResponseBuilder — the model layer has no JSON dependencies.
IAE→400 error contract is explicit and tested (WrapHandlerTest) — error propagation across the api/exploration seam is predictable.
ExploreResponseBuilder.formatAdjectiveProfile and formatSeedCollocates return new maps rather than mutating inputs — the pure conversion style is consistent.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The main issue is a mutable-map seam: buildBaseExploreResponse and populateExploreResponse both operate on caller-mutated maps, creating an implicit contract that handlers must know which fields each helper adds and which remain for them to fill in.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase shows well-designed seams in several places (ValidatedExploreRequest, ExploreResponseBuilder isolation, IAE error contract). What pulls the score down is the mutable-map assembly pattern: response construction is split between buildBaseExploreResponse, populateExploreResponse, and ad-hoc caller mutations, making the full response schema non-obvious at any single call site. A developer adding a new response field has to trace through multiple methods to verify it won't collide. These are real maintenance costs but contained within the api package, placing the score in the mid-70s.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer's class-level Javadoc explains a non-obvious algorithm with a numbered outline and a concrete 'house' example — a developer encountering exploration for the first time gets real orientation.
Logger.debug calls are guarded (isDebugEnabled or logTopProfiles explicit guard) and log meaningful state, not object dumps.
RelationEdgeType.toString() override documents 'why' the override exists, not just 'what' it does.
GrammarConfigLoader's 'always call with throw' note on missingConfigException is genuinely non-obvious guidance for a builder-exception pattern.
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: Issues are isolated to two method-level Javadocs that restate the method name without adding insight — a minor pattern rather than a systemic commenting style.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase largely avoids AI-generated debt patterns: comments explain rationale rather than restating code, debug logging is disciplined and guarded, there are no defensive null-checks on non-nullable values, and no boilerplate error handling is copied across files. The score is 81 rather than 88+ because two Javadoc comments on ExploreResponseBuilder.buildEdges overloads echo the method name without adding context (acceptable but slightly noisy), and a handful of very short factory methods carry multi-line Javadocs that add modest value over reading the 2-3-line body directly.

strengths: All deprecated symbols are clearly annotated with @Deprecated and carry explicit v2.0 removal timelines in their Javadoc.
Active production code consistently uses the new APIs (2-arg constructors, ExploreOptions overload).
No stale FIXME/TODO migration markers found across the scanned files.
Deprecated constructors are properly isolated — they delegate to the new path with a null argument rather than duplicating logic.
incomplete_migration.issue_character: The migration is well-managed but not yet complete: deprecated fallback code paths remain live, and one 'survivor param' (nounsPerSeed=0) leaks the pre-ExploreOptions API shape into multi-seed callers.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates disciplined deprecation management: symbols are marked, active callers are migrated, and removal timelines are documented. The score is 80 rather than 88+ because the deprecated constructors keep two fallback constants (FALLBACK_NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT, FALLBACK_ADJECTIVE_PATTERN) as live code paths that diverge from config-driven behavior — these create a maintenance surface that grows invisible as v2.0 approaches. The ExploreOptions.nounsPerSeed=0 sentinel for multi-seed callers is a minor but real remnant of the pre-migration API shape.

strengths: All packages have fewer than 11 files; no flat-directory overload anywhere in the codebase.
indexer/blacklab/ is correctly isolated as a 2-file subsystem with no dependencies outside model/utils.

exploration/ cleanly groups the exploration algorithm components (SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator, ExploreOptions) with package-private scoping on the non-public classes.
query/ enforces package-private scoping on CollocateQueryHelper and BlackLabSnippetParser — exactly the right navigability decision.
package_organization.issue_character: The organization is sound except for one misplaced file: RadialPlot belongs to api/visualization concerns but lives in utils/, breaking the utils/-as-general-primitives contract.
package_organization.score_rationale: The package layout is navigable and well-considered: each directory has a clear purpose, file counts are manageable, and scoping decisions (package-private helpers) reinforce the boundaries. The score is 77 rather than 85+ due to RadialPlot's placement in utils/ — it is the only file in the package with no util callers and a domain-specific rendering responsibility. This is a clear misplacement that a developer searching for visualization code will not find in utils/. One additional concern: model/ hosts QueryResults which ties closely to query/ internals (SnippetResult, CollocateResult types are only produced by BlackLabQueryExecutor), but this is a borderline design decision with a reasonable rationale.

strengths: LogDiceCalculator.compute documents the UNCOMPUTABLE sentinel and callers consistently check Double.isNaN — the exception/sentinel contract is clean end-to-end.
ExplorationResult.empty() enforces its non-null seed contract at runtime with requireNonNull — the documented precondition is actively enforced.
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish's IOException-to-IllegalStateException wrapping is explicitly documented with the config path in the message, making startup failures immediately actionable.

QueryExecutor.findCollocations documents the IllegalArgumentException contract for malformed cqlPattern, and the implementation honors it — the interface-level contract matches the implementation behavior.
contract_coherence.issue_character: Issues are localized to two cases where a method's declared contract (buildFullPattern @Nullable, orElseThrow on already-guarded Optional) does not match what callers can assume at their specific call sites — creating a correctness gap that is non-obvious without tracing through multiple methods.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase has strong contract discipline in its core utilities (LogDiceCalculator, ExplorationResult, GrammarConfigLoader) and consistent exception handling. The score falls to 78 because buildFullPattern's @Nullable annotation creates a documented contract that active callers violate (no null check before use), and handleSemanticFieldExplore's orElseThrow on an already-guarded Optional creates an implicit dependency on the validation path that a reader cannot see at the call site. These are correctness risks, not theoretical concerns: the buildFullPattern null path would produce NPE rather than a 400 if a null-pattern relation slipped through loading.

strengths: Guard clauses at method entry are consistent across all five QueryExecutor implementations: null/empty lemma → early return with empty list.
The four-phase exploration algorithm in SemanticFieldExplorer delegates each phase to a named private method, making the overall flow readable as an ordered sequence.
wrapWithErrorHandling in HttpApiUtils is a clean, single-responsibility dispatcher: IAE→400, IOException→500, Exception→500, with no overlapping branches.
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens handles nested brackets and quoted strings with explicit depth counting — the logic is provably correct and the method is narrow in scope.
ConlluConverter.convertConlluToWplChunks documents the deliberate choice to leave a partial sentence open on the error path, preventing misreads of the finally block.
logic_clarity.issue_character: Issues are isolated and mostly cosmetic — one real control-flow inconsistency (internal catch inside a wrapped handler), one always-true null guard, one misleading split declaration — rather than a systemic logic problem.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase scores well on the core logic_clarity rubric: no identical if/else branches, no dead code after unconditional returns, no always-false conditions, consistent guard-clause discipline. The score is pulled below 80 by the handleRelationQueryForPattern catch that contradicts both the method's throws declaration and the wrapHandler contract (a developer reading the method signature expects IOExceptions to propagate, but they don't), and the redundant null guard on a non-nullable record component. These are minor in count but non-trivial to reason about — a developer debugging an unexpected 500 message format will be confused by the two divergent paths.

strengths: Systematic use of @Nullable from org.jspecify on method return types and parameters throughout the API and query layers.
Optional<RelationType> in RelationConfig encodes the absent-type case in the type system rather than using null or a sentinel string.
All callers of @Nullable-returning helpers (requireParam, resolveRelationConfig) null-check the result immediately, showing the annotation contract is respected.
No unchecked casts or raw generic types found in production code.
Fastjson2 typed getters (getString, getIntValue, getBooleanValue) prevent silent type coercion bugs.
type_safety.issue_character: Issues are concentrated in declaration gaps — @Nullable is used but not universally, and one constructor annotation contradicts its class's actual null-tolerance — rather than a fundamental absence of type discipline.
type_safety.score_rationale: The codebase shows deliberate type discipline: @Nullable annotations, consistent Optional usage, and typed JSON accessors. Two concrete gaps pull the score below 80: the misleading @Nullable on CollocateQueryHelper's constructor (the class cannot actually function with a null index), and the undeclared nullable field in CollocateResult whose callers nevertheless check for null. A codebase at 80 would have these declarations aligned with observed runtime behavior across all public boundaries; this one has a few mismatches that require reading implementation code to understand true nullability.

strengths: QueryExecutor method names precisely encode both operation type and query format (executeSurfacePattern vs executeBcqlQuery vs executeCqlQuery) — a developer can choose the correct method without reading docs.
Handler class names accurately predict their scope: SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, VisualizationHandlers — no false advertising.
Record component names in model classes are self-documenting: CoreCollocate.sharedByCount, DiscoveredNoun.combinedRelevanceScore, AdjectiveProfile.commonalityScore.
RelationType enum values (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF) are precise domain terms.
ExploreResponseBuilder method names accurately describe output structure: formatSeedCollocates, formatDiscoveredNouns, formatCoreCollocates, buildEdges.
naming_quality.issue_character: Issues are scattered local mismatches — a misleading method Javadoc, an abbreviated variable name, a misnomer in a record field — rather than pervasive vocabulary drift or systematic inconsistency.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates above-average naming discipline in its public API layer (QueryExecutor, model records, handler classes). The score is pulled below 80 by three meaningful naming defects: ExploreOptions.nounsPerSeed misrepresents the controlled variable (nouns per collocate, not per seed), BlackLabSnippetParser.extractCollocateLemma's name promises label-aware extraction it doesn't perform, and the 'colloc' vs 'collocate' abbreviation split across the exploration subsystem creates friction when reading code that spans those files.

strengths: wrapWithErrorHandling creates a single, testable error boundary for all registered handlers — the mapping rule is declared once and tested in WrapHandlerTest.
All wrap-caused error responses use JSON with an 'error' key — consistent response shape for clients.
Exception wrapping always preserves the original cause (new IOException(msg, cause), new IllegalStateException(msg, cause)) — debuggable stack traces throughout.
Intentional partial-error collection in executeRelationQueries is clearly demarcated: errors go to 'errors' key in the response, normal results to 'relations' key.
error_consistency.issue_character: Issues stem from one pre-existing internal catch that was not removed when wrapHandler was introduced, and one error message that drops the bad parameter's name — isolated inconsistencies rather than a systemic pattern.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The error-handling framework is well-designed: a canonical wrapper, IAE-as-validation-error convention, cause-preserving wraps at layer transitions. The score is held below 80 primarily by the handleRelationQueryForPattern catch that contradicts the wrapHandler contract — the 500-message format diverges from all other handlers and the logger level (error vs warn) also diverges. The missing parameter name in the NumberFormatException message is a smaller gap. A codebase scoring 80 would have zero handlers catching exceptions before wrapHandler gets a chance, except at intentional partial-result boundaries.

strengths: Port binding explicitly deferred to start() — construction and binding are correctly separated.
CORS system property read at response time, not class-load time — documented with rationale.
Shutdown hook registered before server construction — executor cleanup is guaranteed even on construction failure.
No global mutable singletons with cross-module ordering dependencies.
Static state consists only of immutable constants (Map.of, compiled Patterns, numeric values) — safe at any load order.
initialization_coupling.issue_character: There are no material initialization_coupling defects in this codebase; Java's classloading and constructor discipline is consistently applied. The only minor concern is a @Nullable annotation on a parameter that cannot safely be null at runtime.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: Java's static typing and explicit constructors make module-level side effects like Python import-time I/O structurally impossible. Within that context, this codebase applies the right patterns: deferred binding, call-time property reads, DI through constructors. The score is 86 rather than higher because the @Nullable on CollocateQueryHelper's constructor creates a misleading impression that null is a valid initialization input, and no runtime guard validates it — a developer adding a test or extending the class could accidentally pass null and trigger NPEs without violating any declared contract.

strengths: Java naming conventions (case, visibility) are uniformly applied with zero drift — no camelCase/snake_case mixing, no inconsistent constant formats.
Package organization has a clear, documented separation of concerns with no files in surprising locations (after the recent RadialPlot/LongIntHashMap moves).
The mixed record/class model design is explicitly documented in package-info.java with a stated rationale.
Deprecated API evolution follows a consistent two-overload pattern with versioned removal notes in both places it appears.
convention_outlier.issue_character: Issues are isolated convention drifts within the exploration package and the API response layer — one public/package-private inconsistency, one inline vs. delegated response-building inconsistency — rather than file-organization or naming-convention drift.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase largely follows consistent conventions. The score is pulled below 80 by two concrete outliers: CollocateProfileComparator being public while its sibling MultiSeedExplorer is package-private (without documented rationale), and handleSemanticFieldComparison building its response inline while its three sibling handlers in the same class delegate to ExploreResponseBuilder — a visitor to that class will see three handlers that look one way and one handler that looks another way, which is the definition of a style island.

strengths: Pure computational units (CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, LongIntHashMap, BlackLabSnippetParser) are thoroughly unit-tested in isolation.
New exploration code added in round 16 has immediate test coverage — WrapHandlerTest, ExploreResponseBuilderTest, MultiSeedExplorerTest.
Test isolation via stub executors is used consistently — no test depends on a live corpus.
No snapshot overuse — all tests assert specific values or behaviors.
test_strategy.issue_character: Coverage gaps are concentrated in the HTTP handler layer (API endpoint tests) and the CLI entry point — the integration glue between components is untested at the unit level, leaving several code paths reachable only through full integration tests that require an external index.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The unit test layer for pure logic is solid (scoring at or above 80 for that subsystem). The overall score is dragged down significantly by the absence of handler-level tests for several important endpoints (handleSemanticFieldComparison, handleFullSketch, handleDepSketchForType), no test for the CLI argument parsing in Main, and no test for the handleRelationQueryForPattern internal error path whose inconsistency has been identified across multiple review dimensions. A codebase at 80 would have stub-based handler tests for all public endpoint paths, with at least error and happy-path coverage.

strengths: QueryExecutor parameter ordering is fully consistent across all six methods — a developer can learn the pattern from one method and apply it to all.
HttpApiUtils exchange-first convention is applied uniformly across all 7 exchange-bearing utility methods.
SemanticFieldExplorer preferred API (3-param with ExploreOptions) is coherent and parallel across the single-seed and multi-seed paths.
Endpoint documentation in WordSketchApiServer Javadoc matches the actual registered routes.
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: Issues are narrow but affect caller experience: the 'top' vs 'limit' naming drift between GET and POST endpoints, the divergent primary-result array key names, and the exposed 4-param internal overload — these are the kind of inconsistencies that show up as one-off special cases in client code.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The Java API layer (QueryExecutor, SemanticFieldExplorer) is coherent with consistent parameter ordering and clear deprecation paths. The HTTP API surface is less coherent: 'top' vs 'limit' for the same concept, different array key names across endpoints, and a lower-level overload that leaks implementation detail into the public SemanticFieldExplorer API. A score of 80 would require consistent max-results parameter naming across all endpoints and the lower-level pattern-string overload to be package-private.

strengths: Loopback-only binding is a structural enforcement of the security perimeter — no firewall rule or auth layer can be misconfigured to expose it.
CORS headers are applied by every response path, not selectively — no response can escape without the appropriate CORS origin header.
Input validation (body size, pattern length, bracket depth) is applied consistently on all POST endpoints.
All endpoints follow the same auth model (none) — there are no partially-protected routes that would indicate an incomplete implementation.
authorization_consistency.issue_character: For a local read-only research tool, the authorization model (open on loopback, CORS-gated) is appropriate and consistently applied. There are no partial-auth issues because there is no auth to be partial about.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: The authorization model is fit-for-purpose and applied without gaps or inconsistencies. The score is 85 rather than higher because the security assumptions (loopback-only, no auth) are not explicitly documented as intentional decisions — they are code-level facts a developer could change without understanding the security rationale. A codebase scoring 90+ would have a security model section in the README or server Javadoc explaining why loopback binding is the security perimeter and what the threat model is for a shared-machine deployment.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern's 4-phase decomposition into focused private helpers is a model of single-responsibility method design.
GrammarConfigLoader private-method decomposition (parse, parseAndValidateVersion, validateNoLegacyKeys, parseRelations, parseRelation, validatePositions) is thorough and each helper is narrow.
CollocateQueryHelper two-phase executeBcqlQuery (collectHits → scoreHits) clearly separates data collection from scoring.
BlackLabQueryExecutor.collectFrequenciesFromGroups abstract parameterisation avoids duplication across four query methods without artificial inheritance.
SemanticFieldExplorer acting as a narrow facade over MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator is a clean separation — callers get a single entry point, internal classes own their algorithms.
design_coherence.issue_character: Design issues are concentrated at the boundaries between layers: one monolithic method that needs decomposition (MultiSeedExplorer.explore), one class that has outgrown its name (SketchHandlers), and one handler that bypasses the presentation layer established by ExploreResponseBuilder.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase has genuinely good design in its query and config layers. The score is held at 70 by three structural issues that affect maintainability: MultiSeedExplorer.explore is a ~90-line monolith performing 5 distinct operations without helpers (the single-seed counterpart has 4 named helpers for similar complexity); SketchHandlers accumulates sketch, concordance, and BCQL concerns under a misleading class name; and handleSemanticFieldComparison breaks the ExploreResponseBuilder delegation that all other exploration handlers respect. These aren't catastrophic — the code is correct — but they create real friction for a developer extending or debugging the system.

issues.summary: RadialPlot is in utils/ but is an SVG renderer only consumed by api/VisualizationHandlers
issues.suggestion: Move RadialPlot.java into the api/ package (it is already package-internal in practice) or create a utils/viz/ sub-package. Update the single import in VisualizationHandlers.java. No functional change needed.
evidence: RadialPlot imports nothing from utils/ and provides no generic utility. It renders SVG collocate diagrams.
The only caller is VisualizationHandlers.java in the api package: `RadialPlot.renderFromItems(center, items, width, height, mode)`.
Placing rendering logic in utils/ makes a developer searching for visualization code look in the wrong package.

issues.summary: WordSketchApiServer constructs SemanticFieldExplorer directly, eliminating an injection seam
issues.suggestion: Accept SemanticFieldExplorer as a constructor parameter in WordSketchApiServer, or accept it as a parameter to the ExplorationHandlers factory. Construction can be moved to Main.java where all other wiring happens.
evidence: WordSketchApiServer constructor: `SemanticFieldExplorer semanticFieldExplorer = new SemanticFieldExplorer(executor, grammarConfig);` — the api layer constructs the exploration-layer object inline.

SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers accept their collaborators via constructor injection; WordSketchApiServer itself does not (QueryExecutor and GrammarConfig are injected, but SemanticFieldExplorer is not).
This makes it impossible to substitute a mock or alternative SemanticFieldExplorer in integration tests that stand up the API server.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer is now a near-empty facade that obscures the exploration subsystem structure
issues.suggestion: Either (a) elevate MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator to public classes so callers can choose the right abstraction level directly, or (b) consolidate the pre-condition checks and routing logic so SemanticFieldExplorer adds visible value beyond delegation (e.g., unified error normalization, option validation, caching). If (a), SemanticFieldExplorer can be removed.
evidence: exploreMultiSeed(Set, RelationConfig, ExploreOptions) body: `return multiSeedExplorer.explore(seeds, relationConfig, opts.minLogDice(), opts.topCollocates(), opts.minShared());` — a pure pass-through.
compareCollocateProfiles enforces `size() < 2` guard then delegates: `return comparator.compareCollocateProfiles(seedNouns, minLogDice, maxPerNoun);`.

Both MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator are package-private (correct scoping), so SemanticFieldExplorer IS the public API — but its value beyond routing is minimal now that the deleted delegates have been removed.

issues.summary: RelationType enum in model/ conflates parsing-strategy markers (SURFACE, DEP) with semantic relation roles (ADJ_PREDICATE, SUBJECT_OF…)
issues.suggestion: Split into two enums: `QueryStrategy { SURFACE, DEP }` in config/ for routing, and `SemanticRelation { ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF }` in model/ for semantic typing. RelationConfig can carry both. This aligns enum ownership with the layer that uses it.
evidence: RelationType values: SURFACE, DEP (corpus-query routing markers), ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF (semantic relation types used by exploration and response-building).

GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType parses all six from JSON. SketchHandlers.handleRelationsForType filters by SURFACE or DEP. CollocateProfileComparator.deriveAdjectivePattern filters by ADJ_MODIFIER or ADJ_PREDICATE. These are two entirely different semantic uses of the same enum.
A future addition of a new corpus-query strategy (e.g., DEPENDENCY_V2) would touch the same type as a new semantic relation (e.g., GENITIVE_OF), creating confusing co-evolution.

issues.summary: QueryExecutor interface has 7 methods and only one production implementation, imposing interface overhead without current payback
issues.suggestion: Consider splitting QueryExecutor into two narrower interfaces: CollocateQueryPort (findCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeBcqlQuery) used by exploration, and DependencyQueryPort (executeDependencyPattern, executeDependencyPatternWithPos) used by sketch handlers. This makes each interface testable at a finer granularity and makes dependencies explicit. Alternatively, accept the single-impl design as a testability shim and document it explicitly.
evidence: QueryExecutor declares findCollocations, executeCqlQuery, executeBcqlQuery, getTotalFrequency, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern, executeDependencyPatternWithPos — 7 methods.
Only BlackLabQueryExecutor implements these in production. The interface is used for test mocking only.
The interface's Javadoc correctly explains each method's distinct responsibility, but 7 abstract methods is a large surface for a one-implementation abstraction.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer leaks a 5-parameter low-level exploreByPattern overload as a public method alongside the RelationConfig overload
issues.suggestion: Reduce the 5-param overload to package-private (remove `public`) so it serves only as an internal implementation step. If callers outside the exploration package need low-level control, document that explicitly. This removes the API leakage without changing behavior.
evidence: Two public overloads: `exploreByPattern(String seed, RelationConfig config, ExploreOptions opts)` and `exploreByPattern(String seed, String relationName, String bcqlPattern, String simplePattern, ExploreOptions opts)`.
